coredata.dc:title: Giant energy storage capacity of graphene quantum dots prepared by facile method
coredata.dc:creator: Suganya, G
Arivanandhan, M
Kalpana, G
coredata.dc:description: Supercapattery technology focuses on high specific capacity with both high energy density and power density. In the present work, the electrochemical performance of Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is compared with graphene oxide (GO). GO is synthesized by the modified Hummer's method. Less than 5 nm size of GQDs are synthesized by a facile chemical cutting method. High frequency vibrational mode of sp-sp 2 bonded carbon at 2198 cm−1 is revealed from Raman analysis. The energy gap of GQDs of different sizes was calculated using the Quantum Espresso code based on Plane-Wave Self-Consistent Field (PWSCF). The forbidden gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels with the size of the GQDs cluster. The electrochemical results show that GQDs exhibit pseudocapacitor behavior with high specific capacity and energy density of 1516.7C/g and 425.9 Whkg−1 respectively at current density of 4 A/g. This is very high compared to the electric double layer capacitor (EDLC) behavior (115C/g) of GO. These results illustrate enhanced electrochemical performance, electrical conductivity and ion transport rate implying that the material can facilitate the movement of ions within the device, allowing for fast charging and discharging.

head.ce:title: Giant energy storage capacity of graphene quantum dots prepared by facile method

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapattery technology focuses on high specific capacity with both high energy density and power density. In the present work, the electrochemical performance of Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is compared with graphene oxide (GO). GO is synthesized by the modified Hummer's method. Less than 5 nm size of GQDs are synthesized by a facile chemical cutting method. High frequency vibrational mode of sp-sp 2 bonded carbon at 2198 cm−1 is revealed from Raman analysis. The energy gap of GQDs of different sizes was calculated using the Quantum Espresso code based on Plane-Wave Self-Consistent Field (PWSCF). The forbidden gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) levels with the size of the GQDs cluster. The electrochemical results show that GQDs exhibit pseudocapacitor behavior with high specific capacity and energy density of 1516.7C/g and 425.9 Whkg−1 respectively at current density of 4 A/g. This is very high compared to the electric double layer capacitor (EDLC) behavior (115C/g) of GO. These results illustrate enhanced electrochemical performance, electrical conductivity and ion transport rate implying that the material can facilitate the movement of ions within the device, allowing for fast charging and discharging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Advanced energy storage mechanisms such as supercapattery, which combine the supercapacitive and battery behavior with high power and energy density respectively, are becoming increasingly important as renewable energy sources like wind, geothermal, solar cell, hydroelectricity, rechargeable battery, and fuel cells become more prevalent [1]. Supercapacitors store energy through a non-Faradaic mechanism called electrostatic charge storage. This involves the separation of charges when the electrolyte and the contact between the electrodes, form an electrical double layer [2,3]. Nevertheless, the electrolyte and the electrode, which can store energy as ions at the surface of electrode, are generally involved in these redox processes. However, as mentioned, they often have lower conductivity, cycle lifetime, and energy density than rechargeable battery electrodes [4].

The combination of supercapacitors and rechargeable batteries can indeed create a higher energy storage device called a supercapattery [1]. The combined effect of supercapacitive with high power density and battery with high energy density form a supercapattery is effectively a hybrid energy storage application. This combination allows for a device that can provide high power bursts and quick charging capabilities, while also provide long-term energy storage [1]. The energy storage technology known as a supercapacitor, frequently referred to as an ultracapacitor, is perfect for high power applications since it can store and release energy quickly [5]. Due to their low energy density, they are unable to store significant amounts of long-lasting energy. While supercapacitors may release energy much more quickly than rechargeable batteries, the former has a far higher energy density and can store energy for longer periods of time [6,7]. By combining these two technologies, a supercapattery can provide both high power and high energy storage capabilities. This makes them useful for applications such as electric vehicles, renewable energy storage, and backup power systems. High power and energy density were recently reported in materials such as Ce/PANI/Fe3O4 [7], rGO-TiO2 [8], CNT/Co3O4/Ag [9], ZnP-rGO [10], NiO/G [11] and Bi2MoO6 [12].

Graphene has been attracting more interest in recent years due to its unusual features. The unique nature of graphene has inculcated more curiosity in recent years. Its honeycomb structure, composed of sp 2-bonded carbon atoms is well ordered in a monolayer, providing it with exceptional mechanical, electrical, and thermal capabilities [13]. Due to its unique characteristics, GO is gaining significance as a potential addition to materials used in supercapacitors. GO has an extremely high specific surface area thereby it exhibits a high specific capacity per unit of mass. Moreover, GO is a low-cost and environmentally friendly material that can be simply and abundantly synthesized from graphite for use in supercapacitors [14]. Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs), nanoscale fragments of graphene typically below 20 nm in size, exhibit unique properties due to edge and quantum confinement effects that distinguish them from larger graphene sheets and reduced graphene oxide. The GQDs consist of one or a few graphene layers with distinct edges often functionalized with groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, or epoxy, [55,56] which enhance electrochemical reactivity by providing active sites for ion interaction and redox reactions crucial in energy storage devices, while their quantum confinement results in discrete energy levels that improve charge storage and transfer, and their edge states differing between zigzag and armchair types contribute variably to the electronic density of states. [57,58] The abundant functional groups at edges and on basal planes enhance dispersibility and act as ion anchoring sites, beneficial for ion storage in supercapattery electrodes, where pseudocapacitance arises from reversible faradaic reactions at the GQD surface, aided by high intrinsic electron mobility from the sp
2 -hybridized carbon lattice structure, which in turn improves conductivity, reduces internal resistance, and enables fast charge-discharge cycles in supercapacitors. Hence, graphene quantum dots are one of the best options for improving advanced energy storage device performance.

In the present work, Hummer's method and chemical cutting (Probe sonication) process were utilized for the synthesis of GO and GQDs respectively, which were then tested as working electrodes for supercapattery devices. XRD and TEM techniques were incorporated to analyze the material's crystal structure, surface morphology and topography. Raman spectroscopy techniques were used to analyze the chemical structure and molecular interactions present in materials. These properties are largely determined by the sp-sp
2 hybridization of carbon atoms in the GQDs. The sp-sp
2 hybridization in GQDs refers to the coexistence of polynic (CC) and cumulenic (C=C) species with sp
2 -hybridized carbon atoms. This hybridization gives rise to a range of structural and vibrational properties that can be characterized using techniques such as Raman spectroscopy. In the case of GQDs, Raman spectroscopy has shown the presence of two main bands: the G and D bands, which are typical of sp
2 -amorphous carbons. These bands appear in the spectra between 900 and 1700 cm−1 [15] and are related to the vibrational modes of the graphitic lattice. Moreover, triple bond C bands, in the 1800–2200 cm−1 spectral region [16,17] have also been observed in GQDs. These features are the imprints of the presence of sp-hybridized carbon atoms, originating from stretching mode of -C ≡ C- bonds [14]. GQDs and GO are both carbon-based materials with different structures and properties. In a three-electrode cell setup with 3 M KOH electrolyte, the electrochemical characteristics of these materials can be investigated. Based on the experimental results, it appears that the GQDs have a greater specific capacity and energy density compared to GO. This could be due to the distinctive properties of GQDs, such as their large active surface area, quantum confinement effect, and strong electron confinement. These properties can enhance the electrochemical activity of GQDs as an electrode material in energy storage devices. An excellent capacitance retention (99.3 %) with a coulombic efficiency of 108.77 % was recorded for up to 5000 cycles at a current density of 4 Ag−1. This is the first study to our knowledge on the utilization of sp-sp
2 GQDs as an active material, which could pave a path for the improvement of effective electrochemical storage devices.

Graphite powder 99.8 %, Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), Hydrochloric acid (HCl), Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), and deionized water (DIW) were purchased from Aldrich alpha.

The GO was synthesized following Hummer's method [18]. The following steps were involved in the preparation: Mixing of graphite powder (1 g) and NaNO3 (1 g) with 60 mL of sulfuric acid, while the solution was done under an ice bath and continuously stirred for two hours. KMnO4 (6 g) was slowly added and the temperature was up to 35 °C for the reaction to take place. Then it was diluted with 92 mL of DIW and stirred for 12 h. The temperature was increased to 98 °C. 100 mL of warm water was used in the dilution of the solution and continuous stirring for two hours with 5 mL of H2O2. A chocolate brown color solution is obtained finally. Centrifuging of solution at 10,000 rpm, cleaning a few times with DIW then filtering and drying at 80 °C for 24 h. Finally, the sample was collected as dry powder.

GQDs were synthesized by the chemical cutting probe sonication method [19] following the steps shown in the schematic diagram (Fig. 1 ). In the first step GO (2 g) was treated in concentrated H2SO4 for 1 h in an ice-water bath and subsequently 0.5 g of KMnO4 was added. These conditions facilitated the shearing of the graphene oxide. The reaction mixture was further stirred at room temperature for 2 h and then heated to 70 °C for an additional hour. Following this, 70 mL of DIW was slowly added to the resulting solution. The reaction temperature was then rapidly increased to 100 °C, causing effervescence, and continued for 30 min until all of the KMnO4 was consumed. To quench the reaction, the solution is kept in an ice bath, and 5 mL of H2O2 was added drop-wise followed by the addition of 70 mL of DIW. This process resulted in an instant transformation of the solution to a yellow transparent state. After bringing the solution to room temperature, the mixture was ultrasonicated for 20 min. The pH was adjusted to 12.0 using NaOH with continuous stirring for 15 min, resulting in the solution turning black. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm) multiple times and washed with ethanol and DIW. The sample was then dried in a vacuum oven at 100 °C for 12 h. Finally samples were collected for characterization studies.

The prepared GO and GQDs synthesized were characterized for structural analysis using X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Philips X'Pert), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) (FTIR 7800 A), DRS-UV spectroscopy (JASCOV570 spectroscopy) and Micro Raman spectrometer with Lab RAM ARAMIS Horiba Jobin-Yvon apparatus. The morphology of GQDs was studied by High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM) with EI Tecnai 300.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs electrode and setup
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical characteristics of GO and GQDs were investigated with a three-electrode setup using Pt wire as a counter electrode and Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode. Around 80:10:10 wt% of GO/GQDs active materials (1 mg), PVDF, and activated carbon with a few drops of NMP solvent are mixed together to form a working electrode. The paste was coated onto a Nickel foam current collector and dried at 100 °C for 8 h. To test the electrochemical analysis of the prepared working electrodes, 3 M KOH electrolyte solution was used. Three different techniques were employed: Cyclic Voltammetry (CV), Galvanostatic charge−discharge (GCD), and Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). To investigate the cyclic stability of the prepared GO and GQDs materials. From, the electrochemical studies the specific capacity, energy and power density, and cyclic stability of the prepared GO and GQDs materials were determined.

The electronic structure of graphene quantum dots was studied using density functional theory (DFT) and the Plane-wave Self-Consistent Field (PWSCF) technique implemented in the Quantum Espresso Code. A (2 × 2 × 2) supercell is used for simulation, which has been completely relaxed to minimize the Hellmann-Feynman force on each atom to less than 10−4 Ry/a.u., demonstrating the optimization of the atomic positions for greater accuracy in the simulation. The Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) generalized gradient approximation is used to calculate the exchange-correlation functional, and norm-conserving pseudopotentials with a 45 Ry plane wave kinetic energy cutoff to calculate the pseudopotentials. The Brillouin zone at gamma position is used for electronic structure calculations since zero-dimensional quantum dots are nonperiodic systems, the gamma point of the Brillouin zone was considered for electronic structure. The energy gap between the HOMO and LUMO levels is determined from the energy spectrum, and the size of the quantum dot and localization of electronic ground states closer to boundaries are related to the HOMO and LUMO gaps.

The XRD analysis technique is used determine of the crystal structure, phase purity and crystallinity of materials and XRD pattern of graphite powder, GO and GQDs as shown in Fig. 2 (a & b). XRD pattern of graphite shows the diffraction peak at 2θ of 27.5° corresponding to the (002) plane in agreement with JCPDS number 75–2078. This peak indicates that the graphite powder has a well-ordered crystal structure possessing crystallinity of high degree [18]. The GO sample, shows a diffraction peak at 2θ of 11.43° corresponding to (001) plane in agreement with JCPDS number 41–1487. Graphite (3.341 Å) has been exfoliated into GO and because of this the d-spacing has increased to 7.5 Å. This is due to introduction of functionalities containing oxygen, at the periphery, epoxy and hydroxyl groups between the planes. The XRD pattern of GQDs shows (Fig. 2b) amorphous carbon nature and subsequent exfoliation and treatment led to changes in the pattern [19]. The disappearance of a specific peaks indicates the successful removal of oxygen-containing groups in GQDs. Hence, the amorphous nature of GQDs is attributed to their small size and the low presence of chemical groups and defects, which can lead to variations in the arrangement of carbon atoms and GQDs can have different hybridization states, including sp, sp 2, and sp 3 hybridization. These different hybridization states contribute to the variety of bonding configurations and properties in GQDs.

The Fourier-transform Infrared spectra of GO and GQDs are depicted in Fig. 3 (a & b). The stretching vibrations of the CC bond at 2201.64 cm−1, sp2 hybridized CC at 1563 cm−1 and carboxyl group of C − O peaks at 1082 cm−1 are observed in GQDs. All peaks exhibit shifts from those observed in the precursor GO. Notably, the carbonyl peaks are attenuated in GQDs compared to the precursor GO. Moreover, the OH-related peak was suppressed in the GQD which confirms the reduction of functional groups in the sample.

Fig. 4 (a & b) shows the UV–Vis spectra of two different materials: GO and GQDs. The absorption peak of GO is observed at a wavelength of 270 nm, while the peak for GQDs is red-shifted to 404.07 nm. This shift in peak position is attributed to extended conjugation of the aromatic sp 2 domain (CC bonds) in GQDs. The different electronic transition states in GQDs are due to the anti-bonding and bonding molecular orbitals of carbon domains such as (CC, sp) and (CC, sp
2 ), as well as the functional groups present on the surface edges of the GQDs. These orbitals are responsible for the absorption of light at different wavelengths, leading to the observed differences in the UV–Vis spectra between GO and GQDs. The n-π and π*-π transitions are caused by these functionalization. The π*-π primary transitions are dominant in the regimes of lower wavelength transitions but they are inhibited at higher excitation wavelengths, where they give way to n-p transitions.

From the DRS UV–Vis spectra using a Tauc plot shown in Fig. 3 (a & b), the optical band gap of the GQDs can be determined. The Tauc plot is a graph of (αhν)1/n vs. (hν) given in this eq. (1).
(1)
αhν
n
= A
hν −
E g
n

Based on the given equation, the optical absorption coefficient (α) is related to the frequency of light (ʋ), Planck's constant (h), a constant (A) and the optical bandgap (Eg) of the material being studied. Since n factor varies with respect to nature of the material, we have n value approximately equal to ½ for indirect band gap and 2 for direct band gap materials. In the study, linear fit n = ½, was using indicating that the GQDs studied have a indirect bandgap [24]. The estimated value of the indirect band gap of the GQDs was found to be 1.67 eV, which is lower than that of GO (2.90 eV). These studies show that GQDs have unique electronic properties, which could make them useful in supercapattery applications.

The Raman spectra shown in Fig. 5 for GO and GQDs exhibit several characteristic features of graphitic materials in the wavenumber region of 1000–3000 cm−1. The D band range of 1300–1340 cm−1 is associated with symmetry-breaking at defects and edges, which leads to the activation of this mode. The G-band range of 1495–1595 cm−1 is a result of in-plane CC deformations. The frequencies of the D and G bands reduce as the defect size and density decrease, which is indicative of larger crystallite sizes in graphene. The GO and GQD show D-band behavior due to quantum confinement which is confirmed through the second-order double resonance component of Raman Scattering [14,19]. Raman spectra of the D and G bands in the GQDs and GO are related to the vibrational modes of sp 3 (D-1333 cm−1) and sp 2 (G-1582 cm−1) hybridized carbon atoms, respectively. The broadening of these bands and the redshift observed in GQDs compared to GO is attributed to the restrain applied to the GQDs due to their smaller size. The reduced intensity of the D band and the strong G band observed in GQDs indicate the prevalence of sp 2 hybridization over sp 3 hybridization in these materials. The C band in GQD relates to the stretching vibration of (-C ≡ C-) bonds, with a peak at 2198 cm−1 in sp hybridized in the material. The observed 2D band at 2800 cm−1 is an effect of the second order of the 2D band generated by phonon scattering at GQD boundaries and defects. The ID/IG intensity ratio, calculated by considering the peak intensity, is higher for GQDs (1.13) compared to GO (0.98), indicating a higher degree of disorder and defects in GQDs. GQDs have a higher ID/IG ratio due to an increase in smaller domains although GO has a lower ID/IG ratio indicating a rise in the average size of the crystallized GO nanostructures. The in-plane crystallite size (La) of graphitic planes was calculated using the relationship (2) shown below.
(2)
L a
=
4.35
I D
/
I G
The estimated in-plane crystallite size (La) for GQDs is 3.84 nm which is smaller compared to that of GO with 4.438 nm.

The characteristic HRTEM images of GQDs at different magnifications were shown in Fig. 6 (a & b). The HR-TEM images of GQDs in Fig. 6c demonstrate that the lattice spacing structures of the GQDs are clearly discernible. The GQDs were found to have sizes ranging from 1 to 5 nm, respectively with the average diameter of approximately 0.24 nm. SAED and EDX of GQDs are shown in Fig. 6(d) and Fig. 6(e), indicates that the GQD has a hexagonal lattice structure (Fig. 6d), which is consistent with the expected crystal structure of graphene. The EDX analysis confirms the presence of only two elements in GQDs, carbon (C) and oxygen (O), with a higher content of carbon than oxygen as shown in Fig. 6e. The presence of oxygen is due to oxygen-containing functional groups even after the reduction of graphene oxide. The higher carbon content implies that the sp 2 carbon conjugated network was restored due to the use of H2SO4 solution, which may have been damaged during the synthesis process. Furthermore, the presence of multiple defects included at the GQDs edges can increase the sites that are responsible for ion diffusion within the GQDs and the electrolyte. GQDs can be used as best electrodes for supercapattery applications since they can provide more paths for charge transmission into interspace.

Using the Plane wave self-consistent field (PWSCF) method [20], which is a feature of the Quantum Espresso code, the electronic structure of hexagonal GQDs was determined. GGA-PBE [21] was used for the exchange-correlation part. The norm-conserving pseudopotentials with a 45 Ry plane wave kinetic energy cutoff to calculate the pseudopotentials. We relax our systems structurally until the force on each atom is less than 0.001 Ry/Bohr. To prevent interactions between GQDs in adjacent supercells, a vacuum distance of 10 Å is applied in all directions. The electronic band structure of graphite was originally calculated [22] for the optimized lattice constant of a = 3.20 Å, and identified to be metallic, which is similar to the experimental result of graphite as well as other theoretical studies [23]. In the present study, the crystal structure and size of GQDs structure, charge density and density of states (DOS) of Graphene, GO and GQDs26 cluster with three distinct sizes were examined. Fig. 7 (a-h) shows the computed crystal structure and different sizes of GQDs structure, charge density and DOS plots of Graphene, GO and GQDs26 cluster. Assuming that of the quantum dots (GQDs26) have a hexagonal shape, their effective size (deff) were calculated using the formula as reported and given in Table 1 [24–26]. As shown in Fig. 6(a), carbon atoms are tightly packed into a hexagonal honeycomb lattice to form a monolayer of graphene. It is an allotrope of carbon with a bond length of 0.142 nm and sp2 hybridization between the atoms. Fig. 7(d) shows DOS of graphene which depicts its semi metallic nature in agreement with earlier experimental and theoretical reports [27,28]. The most stable GO structure for each oxidation level is shown in Fig. 7(b). To create the carbonyl group (CO), the adsorbed O atoms prefer the edge positions between the C atoms, as seen in Fig. 7(b) with OC bond lengths of 1.475 Å. In Fig. 7(e) the DOS of GO is plotted which shows semiconducting behavior with a bandgap of 2.85 eV in agreement with earlier report [29]. Figs. 7(f) depict cluster and DOS of GQDs26. The graph shows that as the quantum dot size increase, the energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO states decrease. Table 1 lists the energy gap values for Graphene, GO, and GQDs26 clusters. From Table 1, it can be seen that the band gap of GQDs26 are larger than GO. The energy gap value of 1.3 eV for GQDs26 is comparably close to the energy gap of 1.6 eV observed in the UV–vis spectra data of GQDs. This leads to enhancement of the material's specific capacitance making GQDs preferable for supercapattery applications.

Charge density plots are used to determine the charge transfer and nature of chemical bonds between the atoms. The charge density plots of graphene and GO are shown in Fig. 7 (g and h). The charge density maps of GO contain both CC, CH and CO bonds. The contour lines between CC atoms are connected due to the sharing of electrons confirming the covalent nature. The electronegativity of Carbon, Hydrogen and Oxygen are 2.55, 2.20 and 3.4 respectively. Due to the higher electronegativity of Oxygen and Hydrogen compared to Carbon, there is transfer of electrons from Carbon to Oxygen and Carbon to Hydrogen. Consequently, the CO and CH bonding involves both ionic and covalent nature with GO exhibits semiconducting behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CV analysis of GO and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical analysis of the NF [49], Activated Carbon [50], GO and GQDs electrode materials were analyzed using three-electrode system whose schematic is shown in Fig. 8 . The CV curves of working electrodes are obtained by plotting the capacitance with respect to applied voltage at various scan rates of 5–100 mV/s and are shown in Fig. 9a & 9b. The EDLC behavior of GO and the pseudocapacitance nature of GQDs can indeed be analyzed through the shape of CV plots. The capacitor-like behavior of GO arises from its large surface area, abundant functional groups, and electrostatic charge storage mechanisms, while the battery-like behavior of GQDs is attributed to their small size, quantum confinement effects, and redox-active nature. In the case of EDLCs, the charges pile up at the electrode-electrolyte interface, exhibiting capacitance but for pseudocapacitors, the capacitance increases due to Faradaic redox reactions at the electrode active surface. The presence of sigmoidal loops in the CV curves of GQDs indicates the charging mechanism of the pseudocapacitor. Due to the Faradaic redox reaction of the electroactive GQDs it resulted in the reversible storage of charge on the electrode surface. The anodic and cathodic peaks observed in the CV curves correspond to the forward and backward reactions of the redox couple, respectively. The Csp is described in the eq. (3), [7].
(3)
C sp
C g
=
∫ I
dV
2 × 3.6 × m × v
where Csp is the specific capacity (C/g), ∫ I
dV is the integral surface area of the CV curve, v is the various scan rate and m denotes the mass of the active electrode material. There are redox peaks in the CV curves GQDs that correspond to a specific capacity than the GO sample. The increased storage capacity of GQDs could be due to the fact that GQDs possess a greater number of active sites for redox reactions along with the EDLC function. The following are the possible redox reactions for GQDs.

Furthermore, GQDs are composed of confined sheets with very small sizes, resulting in a shorter ionic diffusion length and increased electrochemical active sites. The redox peak current increases with the scan rate and the oxidation peaks shift toward the positive potential whereas the reduction peaks shift toward negative potential respectively contributing to the electrodes resistive effect, polarization and fast ion kinetics [40,41]. It is also possible that the large specific area of the electrode materials and ease of ion transport are responsible for the high current response [44,45]. The current responsiveness of the electrode increases at the electrode/ electrolyte interfaces by redox processes.

The ions use the whole active surface area for charge storage in the low range of scan rates which leads to high specific capacity. The well-reversible Faradaic redox reactions reveal that the electrode material rate capability is remarkable even at high scan rates. According to Fig. 9b, both the peak currents for oxidation and reduction demonstrate a nearly linear relationship with the square root of the scan rate. The quasi-reversible redox process occurs at the electrode/electrolyte interfaces. During OH− diffusion, the distribution of oxidation and reduction can occur among the electrolyte and surface of the electrode respectively. By an optimal selection of GQDs, the honeycomb structure increases the active sites in the electrodes, improving the surface area, conductivity and porosity thereby attracting more ions to the electrode surface, allowing for easy ion transport. The higher electrical polarization of the KOH electrolyte ions may lead to kinetic irreversibility at the electrode/electrolyte interface. This can result in decreased access of the electrolyte ions to the working electrode surface, potentially limiting the electrochemical reaction. However, despite these challenges, the specific capacity of the GQDs electrode was found to be relatively high with 2700.88C/g at a scan rate of 5 mV/s. This value is quite higher than the specific capacity of GO (798.72C/g) at the same scan rate, indicating that GQDs are potential materials for electrochemical applications.

Fig. 9(c) shows the CV curves for both GO and GQDs electrodes with scan rate of 5 mVs−1 in an electrochemical measurement setup. The working potential range for the GO electrode appears to be from −1.2 V to 0.3 V, although the GQDs electrode has a stable working potential window range from 0.0 V to 0.5 V. The combination of battery with supercapacitor mechanism establishes the impact of GQDs in the positive and negative parts. Because, the increased specific capacity owing to the presence of sp. (-C ≡ C-) bond quantum confinement and the large active surface area observed in GQDs during redox reactions may improve the specific capacity even more. The combination of non-Faradaic and Faradaic behavior, however, is shown in CV curves at various scan rates and describes supercapattery behavior. The oxidation and reduction peak currents of GO and GQDs in Fig. 9d exhibit an essentially linear relationship with the square root of the different scan rates using the Randles Sevcik model [42,46]. The quasi-reversible and diffusion-controlled behavior observed at the electrode/electrolyte interfaces with GO and GQDs can be attributed to the facile redox processes involving their unique structural and chemical properties, such as functional groups for GO and edge states for GQDs, which enable rapid electron transfer and ion diffusion across the interface [43,47]. This behavior of GQDs indicates that the redox processes are quasi-reversible and diffusion-controlled, facilitating rapid electron transfer and ion diffusion across electrode/electrolyte interfaces, which is greater in GQDs compared to GO.

The charge storage kinetic analysis was conducted using a series of CV measurements at different scan rates [41]. Fig. 9 (a & b) present the stepwise CV curves of GO and GQDs electrodes obtained at scan rates ranging from 5 to 100 mV/s, respectively. The observed slight shift in the redox peaks with increasing scan rates suggests rapid reaction rates. Since the electrodes exhibit nanoscale structural formation and an enhanced large surface area, the combined effect of charge storage through Faradaic reactions and non-Faradaic surface charge accumulation defines the pseudo-capacitor-like working mechanism. To understand the charge storage mechanism of the electrodes, Dunn's method was utilized, as denoted by the power law equation presented in Eqs. (4) and (5) [41,48].
(4)
i = a
ν b
(5)
log i = log a + b log ν
where b is determined by the slope of log (I) vs. log (v) plots for redox peaks and a and b are adjustable variables. The b value of 0.5 indicates a diffusion-controlled process, while b value of 1 means a capacitive charge storage process. The calculated b values of the two redox peaks (GO and GQDs) are 0.50 and 0.88, respectively (Fig. 10 (a & b)), suggesting that for this particular GQDs redox process, a mixture of diffusion-controlled and capacitor-like responses occurs upon reduction, with pseudocapacitive reaction dominance compared to GO.

To further quantify the contribution of the charge storage mechanism, a series of CV experiments at slow scan rates were conducted. Eqs. (6 and 7) [51,52] can be applied in this context to distinguish between the capacitive and diffusion behavior within the CV plot. Here, the summation of k1v and k2v1/2 delineates the capacitive and diffusion-controlled contributions to the current as a function of voltage.
(6)
I
V
=
k 1
v +
k 2

(7)

k 1

1 / 2

k 2

By plotting I(V)/v1/2 vs. v1/2, a best-fit line connects the data points from which values of k1 and k2 are representative of slope and y-intercept respectively. Carrying out this process at various potentials provides a separate profile for both capacitive and diffusion processes. Once the areas of k1 and k2 values are known, the charge transfer current Id can be easily obtained by multiplying the desired scan rate. In Fig. 10 (c & d), the capacitive nature becomes more apparent as the scan rate increases. The capacity distribution of GO and GQDs electrodes is illustrated in Fig. 10c and d, respectively. At scan rates of 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, and 100 mV/s, GO exhibited capacitive distributions of 16 %, 8.5 %, 3.6 %, 1.8 %, 1.2 %, and 0.9 %,respectively. Conversely, the diffusion current was higher at higher concentrations of GQDs. The GQDs electrode displayed capacitive distributions of 67 %, 74 %, 82 %, 86 %, 88 %, and 90 % at the same scan rates. This can be advantageous for supercapattery, as higher diffusion currents typically translate to improved charge storage capabilities and higher energy densities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GCD analysis of GO and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 11 (a & b) show the electrochemical performance of GO and GQDs electrodes in a three-electrode setup using 3 M KOH electrolyte. Current density ranges from 4 to 12 A/g were used during the evaluation, which was conducted in positive and negative potential windows. The concentration of the electrolyte has a direct effect on the count of ions in the solution that affects the ionic conductivity of the electrolyte. Higher concentrations of the electrolyte result in more ions and higher ionic conductivity. Fig. 11a and Fig. 11b, explain the dominance of different behaviors in distinct potential windows. Fig. 11a illustrates the dominance of EDLC behavior by GO in the negative to positive potential window range (−1.0 to 0.3 V). In contrast, Fig. 10b depicts the dominance of Faradaic behavior by GQDs in the positive potential window from 0.0 to 0.39 V. The Faradaic behavior is owing to the presence of quantum dots, which have a clear sp bonding, as evidenced by the Raman spectra. The confined quantum dots also contribute to the additional pseudocapacitance observed in this range. The curves show two potential drops, a fast one in the range of 0.4–0.25 V and a slow one in the range of 0.25–0.15 V in the Faradic behavior indicating the GQDs electrode. This behavior is consistent with the CV curves. Hence, the GCD curves of GQDs show better performance than GO, which implies that GQDs electrode has a good storing capacity. Based on the discharge curve, Csp of GQDs was estimated by eq. (8):
(8)
C sp
C g
=
I × ∆ t
m × 3.6
where, I- is the discharge current (A), Δt- is the discharge time (s) and m- is the mass (g) of the active electrode material. According to Fig. 11c, the GQDs electrode demonstrates a high specific capacity in the potential window compared to a low specific capacity for GO in the negative potential window at current density of 4 A/g. Hence, GQDs exhibit fast charging and discharging compared to GO. From Fig. 11(d) it appears that the specific capacity of GQDs is higher in the positive window potential compared to GO in the negative window potential. Specifically, GQDs exhibit a specific capacity of 1516.7C/g, attributed to their enhanced specific surface area, which facilitates interaction between the electrode and electrolyte, resulting in improved capacity performance compared to GO with a specific capacity of 115C/g at a current density of 4 A/g. In general, an increase in current density in an electrochemical cell setup develops an electric field, leading to various effects on both the Faradic and non-Faradic processes [30,31]. Furthermore, the observed reduction in specific capacity at high current density can be attributed to several factors. First, during charging and discharging, there is less time for the ions to be intercalated or extracted into or out of the electrode when there is a high current density [32,42]. This results in a lower specific capacity since there are fewer ions being stored or released from the electrode. Second, at high current density, the electrolyte may be unable to completely pass through the electrode material, which can lead to a reduction in the effective surface area available for ion exchange. This can also contribute to a lower specific capacity. Finally, the electrode may experience more severe degradation at high current density due to the higher electrochemical reaction rates, resulting in reduced capacity over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS analysis of GO and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: EIS analysis of a material reveals the electrical properties at interfaces. In the case of supercapattery performance, EIS can provide information on the interfacial electrical properties of GQDs working electrode, this is an essential aspect in determining the supercapattery's efficiency. Frequency spectra of EIS in the range of 0.01 and 100 kHz were recorded. In Fig. 12 (a & b), the Nyquist plots were shown for GO and GQDs electrodes. The plots provide information about the electrode charge transfer resistance (Rct) between electrode surface and electrolyte. In the Nyquist plot, the behavior of Rct in the high-frequency region is represented by a semicircle curve and in low low-frequency region the diffusion-limited process is represented by a straight line. Rct value is proportional to semicircle diameter [33,34]. The behavior of energy storage in GQDs is seen in comparing the Nyquist plots of the GO and GQDs. Fig. 12 (a & b) indicates improved charge transfer kinetics and reduced Rct value of GQDs. This suggests that GQDs improve working electrodes performance in energy storage. The equivalent series resistance for GO and GQDs electrode materials are found to be 1.45 and 1.18 Ω. The calculated Rct value is 0.45 Ω for GQDs electrode. Interestingly, the maximum charge transfer was seen at the GQD electrode when compared with the value of GO (1.5) [35,36]. Finally, the GQDs electrode has the optimum capacitance property for quick electron transmission. Fig. 12c depicts the Bode phase angle plot. The device behaves as an ideal capacitor as the phase angle approaches. In this case, a phase angle reaches almost 80° at 0.1 Hz, which is very close to the ideal behavior.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy- power densities and cyclic stability analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The energy and power densities of established GO and GQDs electrodes are calculated using eqs. (9) and (10) [7,37].
(9)
E =
∆ V ×
C sc
2 × 3.6
Wh kg
(10)
P =
E × 3600
∆ t
W kg
where, Csc (C/g) is specific capacity, ∆V (V) is potential window, ∆t is discharge time. The values indicate that GQDs have a higher maximum energy density of 425.9 Wh/kg and a higher power density of 1123.2 W/kg at a current density of 4 A/g compared to GO. The specific capacity of GQDs was calculated using the discharge curve, potential window after IR drop and discharge time. These values are represented in a Ragone plot Fig. 13a shows the energy density and power density connection of the electrodes. Eq. 11 derived from GCD curves was utilized to calculate the energy efficiency of the device [53,54].
(11)
Energy efficie n cy =
Energy density
discharge
Energy density
charge
× 100 %

The energy efficiency of the device was also determined from the GCD curve using Eq. (11), and is found to be 94.76 % at a current density of 12 A/g. The plot for GQDs indicates that as the current density increases, the energy efficiency also increases compared to GO. This relationship is illustrated in Fig. 13b with a plot between current density and energy efficiency.

To evaluate the performance of GO and GQDs namely cyclic stability and coulombic efficiency, continuous GCD cycles were performed for 5000 cycles at a current density of 4 A/g (Fig. 14 (a & b). As described in Fig. 14 (a & b) GQDs showed capacity stability and coulombic efficiency of 99.3 % and 108.77 % respectively after 5000 cycles revealing high stability of cyclic life compared to the GO (79.44 and 107.34). The first cycle, last cycle, as well as number of cycles (inset), are presented in Fig. 14 (c and d), revealing that the shapes of the GCD cycle also maintained their original shape, demonstrating the superior long-term cycle stability of GQDs compared to GO. Table 2 presents the supercapattery performance of GQDs and compares them with earlier reported values for various Graphene-based electrode materials, which clearly indicates the superior performance of GQDs as electrode material.

The quantum confinement effect in GQDs results in discrete energy levels, enhancing electron mobility and storage capacity. Edge types influence the electronic density of states near the Fermi level, thereby affecting conductivity and electron transport. Surface properties, particularly the presence of functional groups, significantly impact GQD reactivity and ion interaction. These functional groups enhance ion anchoring on the GQD surface, which is crucial for both supercapacitor and battery applications. Functionalized edges provide additional sites for pseudocapacitive reactions, directly increasing energy storage capacity. A stable structure that minimizes unwanted reactivity can improve cycle life, reduce charge-discharge time, allow fine-tuning of charge storage capacity and efficiency, enhance conductivity, and minimize internal resistance in batteries, resulting in faster and more efficient energy storage.

GO and GQDs were prepared by modified Hummer's method and chemical cutting probe sonication method, respectively. The successfully synthesized GO and GQDs were confirmed by XRD, FTIR, DRS-UV, Raman spectroscopy and TEM with EDS analysis. The three-electrode system studies showed the redox behavior of GQDs in KOH (3 M) at ambient temperature. The improved performance of sp-sp
2 bonded C in GQDs is attributable to its distinctive quantum dot structure, which provides a large surface area with high redox active sites, and charge transport channels. This confirmed the battery and supercapacitor behavior of GQDs with high specific capacity value of 1516.7C/g compared to GO (115C/g) at 4 A/g. The high energy density and power density of GQD were found to be 425.9 Wh/kg and 1123.2 W/kg, respectively. GQDs better long-term stability was demonstrated by their high-capacity retention of 99.3 % after 5000 cycles than GO (79.44 %). GQDs are an excellent material for supercapattery applications because of all these features.

G Suganya: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. M Arivanandhan: Validation, Supervision, Resources. G Kalpana: Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources.
